rulings.Case Title: ALAN COOKE et al VS. KIN FAT LAM et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Pltf'S To Provide Substative Responses In Compliance With Prior Agreement
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, July 7, 2014, Line 1, DEFENDANT CHARLES HULL Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Pltf'S To Provide Substative Responses In Compliance With Prior Agreement Pro Tem Judge Douglas Robbins, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The tentative ruling exceeds the system character limits. The Pro Tem Judge will email the parties and/or their counsel the tentative ruling prior to 3 pm. If any party does not receive the tentative ruling by 3 pm, please email the Pro Tem Judge at drobbins@woodrobbins.com and cc: all other parties. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to drobbins@woodrobbins.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/pjt

rulings.Case Title: DANIEL LONG VS. FORTY NINERS FOOTBALL COMPANY, LLC, A DELAWARE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Intention To Move For New Trial
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar on Monday, July 7, 2014, Line 8, PLAINTIFF DANIEL LONG's Motion For New Trial. The motion for new trial is denied. The court did not err by finding that the doctrine of equitable tolling did not apply to this case. Plaintiff did not act in good faith and reasonably. Plaintiff had a viable state court action that was litigated for two years and about to go to trial. It was prejudicial to defendant and not in good faith for plaintiff to dismiss that case on the eve of trial, then attempt to litigate the matter in federal court, and then subsequently file a repetitive second state court action. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: KENDRA L. TANACEA VS. RICHARD R. TAVERNETTI MD et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar on Monday, July 7, 2014, Line 9, DEFENDANT EXACTECH, INC.'s DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT. The court overrules the demurrer to the 4th cause of action [strict liability-manufacturing defect]. Paragraphs 34-37 of the amended complaint allege that the manufacturing defect included "material and component degradation, which in turn¿created physical problems within patient's body, such as metal debris¿" and the product differed from defendant's design and specifications. These allegations are sufficient. The court should overrules the demurrer to the 5th cause of action [strict liability-failure to warn]. Paragraphs 42 and 45 of the amended complaint allege that defendant failed to warn about complications/risks for young and physically active persons. The court overrules the demurrers to the 6th cause of action [negligent failure to warn] and 7th cause of action [negligence-manufacturing defect] based on the above. The court sustain the demurrer to the 8th cause of action [br of the implied warranty] without leave to amend. "It is settled law in California that privity between the parties is a necessary element to recovery on a breach of an implied warranty of fitness for the buyer's use¿" Evraets, 29 Cal.App.4th at 788. "Liability on implied warranty has long been recognized, irrespective of negligence, where goods are sold. But (a) this liability is contractual, and requires privity between the person damaged by a defective article and the person liable; (b) timely notice of breach of warranty must be given; and (c) warranties may be excluded by disclaimers in the contract of sale." 6 Witkin Summary Ch. 9, sec. 1428, p. 852. Plaintiff's remedy is to sue in tort and pursue the strict liability causes of action. The court sustains the demurrer to the 9th cause of action [fraud/concealment] with leave to amend. First, plaintiff should allege the c/a with more particularity. While defendant can be liable for Dr. Tavernetti's misrepresentations based on an agency and/or ratification theory, the cause of action is not alleged with the requisite particularity. For example, paragraph 72 of the amended complaint does not allege whether the misrepresentations were oral or in writing. Second, plaintiff should allege the intentional misrepresentation and concealment causes of actions as distinct counts. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: WILLIAM S WEISBERG et al VS. HEIDI TIMKEN et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar on Monday, July 7, 2014, Line 10, DEFENDANTs HEIDI TIMKEN, LESLIE JOHNSON, CHRISTOPHER GONZALEZ and TIMKEN JOHNSON, LLP's MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. No appearances are necessary and no further oral argument is required. The motion is granted. The malicious prosecution cause of action implicates CCP 425.16. Plaintiff is therefore obliged to present evidence to establish the malicious prosecution cause of action. Plaintiff fails to present sufficient evidence showing that the defendants acted with malice. In addition, the court notes that Judge Karnow's order granting summary judgment stated that the motion was granted because the facts were in equipoise. The close call in granting summary judgment indicates that the parties had a legitimate dispute and plaintiff does not sufficiently establish a lack of probable cause. The prevailing party is ordered to prepare a proposed order. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: STEPHEN AMATO-SALVATIERRA VS. JARED FRIEDLAND et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Responses To Requests For Admission, Form Interrogatories, Special Interrogatories, And Requests For Production Of Documents Propounded On Jared Friedland;
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, July 7, 2014, Line 2, PART I OF TENTATIVE RULING, PLAINTIFF STEPHEN AMATO-SALVATIERRA'S Motion To Compel Responses To Requests For Admission, Form Interrogatories, Special Interrogatories, And Requests For Production Of Documents Propounded On Jared Friedland; Pro Tem Judge Douglas Robbins, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: GRANTED IN PART and DENIED IN PART. Friedland shall serve verifications of his discovery responses within fifteen days of service of entry of this Order. Amato-Salvatierra's request for sanctions is DENIED. Plaintiff Stephen Amato-Salvatierra ("Amato-Salvatierra") concedes that Defendant Jared Friedland ("Friedland") has responded to Form and Special Interrogatories, to Request for Production of Documents, and to Requests for Admissions, but argues said responses lack verification. Amato-Salvatierra seeks verifications and obliquely refers to some interest in compelling Friedland to amend the responses themselves. But the motion papers are decidedly unclear as to exactly how Friedland's responses are insufficient, and what additional information Amato-Salvatierra seeks. Moreover the movant has provided no Separate Statement to aid the Court. The absence of the Separate Statement is fatal for any request to compel further responses. See Cal. R. Ct. Rule 3.1345; Mills v. U.S. Bank, 166 Cal. App. 4th 871, 893 (2008). But a Separate Statement is not required to compel verification of previously issued discovery responses. See Cal. R. Ct. 3.1345(a)-(b). Friedland argues the motion to compel fails outright because of Amato-Salvatierra's failure to comply with the 16 day notice requirement under Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 1005(b). Amato-Salvatierra concedes he violated this statute. But Friedland waives this argument when he makes substantive arguments to the motion to compel. In re Marriage of Lemen, 113 Cal. App. 3d 769, 779 (1980). Amato-Salvatierra also seeks sanctions. The Discovery Act mandates the prevailing party on motions of the kind here, shall receive sanctions. But "monetary discovery sanctions payable to the opposing party are limited to the opposing party's actual costs, including attorneys' fees." In re Marriage of Niklas, 211 Cal. App. 3d 28, 37 (1989). Here Amato-Salvatierra offers the Court no proof in the form of a declaration or otherwise as to his actual costs and fees. Absent such evidence the Court is without authority to issue a sanctions award.

rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Evon Corporation'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents To Defendant Geoffrey Palermo, Set One, To Compel Production Of Documents, And For Monetary Sanction
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, July 7, 2014, Line 3, Plaintiff Evon Corporation'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents To Defendant Geoffrey Palermo, Set One, To Compel Production Of Documents, And For Monetary Sanction Pro Tem Judge Douglas Robbins, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem has not issued a tentative ruling on this motion. Each party is required to bring a proposed order to the hearing. =302/jpt

rulings.Case Title: EVON CORPORATION VS. JUSTICE INVESTORS,A CALIFORNIA LIMITED PARTNERSHIP et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Evon Corporation'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents To Defendant Portsmouth Square, Inc. Set One, To Compel Production Of Documetns, And For Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, July 7, 2014, Line 3, Plaintiff Evon Corporation'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents To Defendant Portsmouth Square, Inc. Set One, To Compel Production Of Documetns, And For Monetary Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Douglas Robbins, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem has not issued a tentative ruling on this motion. Each party is required to bring a proposed order to the hearing. =302/jpt
